question_title: Will Strait of Hormuz daily ship transits recover to a 7-day average of 30 or more before June 28, 2026?
summary: The Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed with PortWatch showing 0 transits on June 14. While the US-Iran MOU was digitally signed June 14 with formal signing scheduled June 19, traffic has not changed — shipowners are waiting for the signing and further clarity. The IRGC (which operationally controls the strait) hasn't endorsed the deal, insurance is frozen, and mines need clearing. To reach a 7-day MA of 30 by June 27, daily transits would need to ramp from ~0 to 30-50+/day within about a week of the signing — an aggressive timeline even in the best case. Polymarket prices a related question (≥60 by June 30) at 18.5% on high volume. Our lower threshold is partially offset by a tighter deadline. Calibrated Monte Carlo gives 24-28%, while Metaculus-based decomposition gives ~12%. I estimate ~15%, reflecting the real but limited probability that a rapid deal → reopening → traffic surge chain succeeds within the tight window.

factors.description: Strait currently at ~0 transits per PortWatch (June 14 = 0 vessels). 7-day MA needs to leap from near zero to 30 in only 10 remaining days — an enormous gap requiring sustained high daily traffic.

Deal digitally signed June 14, formal signing scheduled June 19 at Burgenstock. Strong political momentum (Trump, Iran FM both confirmed) and massive economic pressure ($435M/day losses for Iran) to reopen.
IRGC has NOT endorsed the deal and declared 'complete closure' June 10-11, just 3-4 days before deal announcement. IRGC operationally controls the strait and its compliance is the binding constraint.
500+ ships waiting in the Gulf creating massive backlog surge potential. If strait opens, demand-side ramp could be very fast (50-100 ships/day) as stranded vessels exit.

Mine clearing, insurance coverage gaps, and safety concerns constrain ramp-up speed even after political reopening. Argus reports industry considers it 'very risky to commence transits.' Bimco says 'several weeks' needed.
Polymarket P(7-day MA ≥ 60 by June 30) = 18.5% on $24.7M volume — a liquid, well-informed market. Our lower threshold (30) is easier but 3-day tighter deadline partially offsets.
Fitch assessed broad Hormuz reopening unlikely before July 2026. Insurance markets remain frozen — no commercial coverage available even under military escort.

PortWatch may systematically undercount traffic due to AIS spoofing/jamming in conflict zone. Current 5-10 vessels/day transiting under escort are showing as 0 on PortWatch — resolution source that defines YES/NO.

reasoning:
## Forecast: **15% probability (YES)**

### Key Reasoning

**The situation:** The Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed for 108 days. PortWatch recorded **0 transits** on June 14 — the very day the deal was announced. Traffic remains unchanged as of June 17.

**What needs to happen for YES:**
1. Deal signed June 19 (scheduled, ~70% likely on time)
2. IRGC complies and physically allows passage (most uncertain — they declared "complete closure" days ago)
3. Mine clearing enables safe transit
4. Insurance/shipowner confidence returns
5. Daily transits ramp from 0 to averaging 30+/day for a full week — all by June 27

**Why it's low (~15%):**
- Zero traffic today, and the 7-day MA is a smoothing function that takes days to respond
- IRGC non-endorsement is the binding constraint
- Mines, insurance, and safety concerns create multi-day implementation delays
- Argus reports the industry considers transits "very risky" even post-deal
- Even the April 17 "reopening" announcement failed to restore traffic

**Why it's not lower (~5%):**
- The deal has strong political momentum (both sides confirmed)
- 500+ ships waiting creates enormous surge potential once gates open
- Lower threshold (30) is well below normal (~94), so even partial reopening could hit it
- Polymarket-calibrated simulation suggests 24-28% for comparable question
- $435M/day losses for Iran create extreme pressure to comply

resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if the IMF PortWatch 7-day moving average of daily transit calls for the Strait of Hormuz chokepoint reaches 30.0 or higher on any single day from June 13, 2026 through June 27, 2026 (UTC dates inclusive), as reported in the PortWatch "Daily Transit Calls and Trade Volume Estimates" dataset for the Strait of Hormuz chokepoint. The data is accessible via the IMF PortWatch platform (https://portwatch.imf.org/), under the Strait of Hormuz chokepoint "Transit Calls" series. A 7-day moving average of, for example, 30.0 transits/day enters as "30". Otherwise the question resolves NO.
fine_print: "7-day moving average" refers to the trailing 7-day arithmetic mean of daily transit calls for the Strait of Hormuz chokepoint as published by PortWatch; if PortWatch does not publish a moving-average field directly, the resolver will compute it as the arithmetic mean of the most recent seven available daily transit-call values for the chokepoint. The threshold of 30.0 applies to the highest such value observed on any date in the window June 13–27, 2026 (UTC). If PortWatch revises its historical values, the most recent values shown on the platform as of June 28, 2026 govern. If PortWatch is unavailable or has not updated its Strait of Hormuz transit-call data for the relevant period by June 28, 2026, the resolver may rely on equivalent daily transit-count figures reported for the Strait of Hormuz by the Joint Maritime Information Center or other [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions); if no such figures are available and PortWatch cannot be consulted, the question resolves N/A.

factors.description: Strait currently at ~0 transits per PortWatch (June 14 = 0 vessels). 7-day MA needs to reach 30 from near zero in 10 remaining days.
Deal digitally signed June 14, formal signing scheduled June 19. Strong political momentum and massive economic pressure to reopen.
IRGC has NOT endorsed the deal and declared 'complete closure' June 10-11, just days before deal. IRGC operationally controls the strait.
500+ ships waiting to transit creating massive backlog surge potential once opened. Could push daily transits to 50-100/day quickly.
Mine clearing, insurance coverage, and safety concerns constrain ramp-up speed even after political reopening. Bimco says 'several weeks' needed.
Polymarket P(7-day MA >= 60 by June 30) = 18.5% ($24.7M volume). Our threshold is lower (30 vs 60) but deadline tighter (June 27 vs 30).
Fitch assessed broad Hormuz reopening unlikely before July 2026. Insurance markets frozen.
PortWatch may systematically undercount traffic due to AIS spoofing/jamming in conflict zone. Current 5-10 vessels/day under escort not showing in PortWatch.

input.anchor: Base rate: ~15-20% implied by Polymarket-calibrated Monte Carlo simulation. Polymarket P(>=60 by June 30)=18.5% as anchor, adjusted for lower threshold (30 vs 60) and tighter deadline (June 27 vs 30). Metaculus decomposition gives ~12%. Central estimate ~15-17%.
input.assessment:
This question asks whether the Strait of Hormuz 7-day moving average of daily ship transits will reach 30+ on any day from June 13-27. Currently traffic is at effectively zero per PortWatch, and June 13-17 are already past with near-zero counts. The formal deal signing is scheduled for June 19, with the MOU calling for "immediate reopening." 

The key tension: the deal exists and there's enormous economic/political pressure to reopen, but the IRGC (which operationally controls the strait) hasn't endorsed it, mines need clearing, insurance is frozen, and traffic hasn't moved since the announcement. Even the optimistic scenario (signing June 19, traffic starts June 20-21) leaves only 6-7 days for the 7-day MA to reach 30 - requiring rapid daily ramp from 0 to 30-50+.

Monte Carlo simulation calibrated against Polymarket data gives 24-28%, while Metaculus-based decomposition gives ~12%. The discrepancy comes from different assumptions about ramp speed. PortWatch undercount in conflict zones adds additional downside. I converge on ~15-17%.
input.calibration_notes: Polymarket anchor at 18.5% for the harder question (>=60 by June 30) suggests our easier threshold but tighter deadline should be roughly similar. The Metaculus 40% for 'reopening' doesn't map directly to 30 7-day MA because reopening ≠ 30 daily transits. Key hedging check: I'm between 12-28% range from different methods - settling near middle at 17% seems reasonable rather than lazy.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Will the June 19 signing actually happen? 2. Will IRGC comply with the deal? 3. How fast can mine clearing enable safe passage? 4. Will PortWatch capture transit activity given AIS disruptions? 5. How fast will shipowners actually start transiting (insurance barrier)?
input.tool_audit: research() provided excellent comprehensive data on the crisis, deal status, and market context. web_search found critical Polymarket data ($24.7M volume market) and Argus media report confirming zero traffic change post-deal. PortWatch direct access wasn't available but third-party trackers (straits.live) confirmed 0 transits on June 14. Metaculus coherence links returned empty. execute_code MC simulation was useful for quantitative analysis and Polymarket calibration.
input.update_triggers: - June 19 signing succeeds or fails; - IRGC publicly endorses the deal; - First major convoy reported transiting; - PortWatch data shows any increase above 0; - Netanyahu escalation derails deal; - Mine clearing operations announced as complete
input.process_reflection: The forecasting system worked well for this question. research() provided outstanding comprehensive summaries saving significant time. The Polymarket data integration was especially valuable as a calibration anchor. The main friction was the timeout on the first comprehensive parameter sweep - had to simplify the grid search. Having a tool that could directly pull PortWatch data would have been very helpful. The simulation approach with Polymarket calibration was a strong methodology - it let me reconcile bottom-up decomposition with market prices.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

### Resolution Criteria
✅ Resolution criteria are fully provided and the agent engages with the specific terms: IMF PortWatch 7-day moving average, June 13-27 window, threshold of 30.0.

### Factor Verification

**Factor 1 (near-zero transits, logit=-1.5):** ✅ Grounded. The trace confirms via straits.live that PortWatch showed 0 vessels on June 14, and the Argus media article confirms "no change" in traffic after the deal announcement.

**Factor 2 (deal signed, political momentum, logit=+1.0):** ✅ Grounded. Trace confirms MOU digitally signed June 14, formal signing scheduled June 19 at Burgenstock Resort, Switzerland. Multiple sources (CBS, NBC, Al Jazeera, CNN) corroborate.

**Factor 3 (IRGC non-endorsement, logit=-1.5):** ✅ Grounded. Research confirms IRGC declared "complete closure" June 10-11 and has not publicly endorsed the deal. Direction correct (pushes toward NO).

**Factor 4 (500+ ships backlog, logit=+0.8):** ✅ Grounded. Argus article explicitly states "More than 500 ships remain in the Mideast Gulf." Direction correct (pushes toward YES).

**Factor 5 (mine clearing/insurance constraints, logit=-1.0):** ✅ Grounded. Argus article confirms "Floating mines remain a major concern" and "Bimco estimates it could take several weeks for all to leave, even after reopening." Direction correct.

**Factor 6 (Polymarket calibration, logit=-0.3):** ✅ Grounded. Polymarket API data shows P(≥60 by June 30) = 18.5%, volume $24,687,822. The claim of "$24.7M volume" matches. Direction correct (slightly pushing toward NO since the Polymarket signal suggests market skepticism even for a much later deadline).

**Factor 7 (Fitch assessment, logit=-0.8):** ✅ Grounded. Worldview research confirms "Fitch assessed broad Hormuz reopening as unlikely before July 2026" and "insurance companies remain largely unwilling to cover Hormuz transit."

**Factor 8 (PortWatch undercount, logit=-0.5):** ✅ Grounded. Straits.live notes 13 tankers running dark and 64 vessels "in transit" not reflected in PortWatch counts. The direction is correct — if PortWatch undercounts, it makes the 30-threshold harder to reach on the resolution source.

### Double-Counting
Minor overlap between Factor 5 (insurance as operational constraint) and Factor 7 (insurance as market signal), but they draw on different evidence and make distinct analytical points. Not a meaningful problem since the logit magnitudes are modest.

### Wrong-Direction Factors
None found. All logit signs match their descriptions correctly.

### Contradictory Assessment
The assessment acknowledges the tension between factor-implied probability (~3.3% from 50% prior) and the final 17% estimate, explaining this is because factors should apply to a Polymarket-calibrated anchor (~18-25%), not to a 50% prior. This is methodologically sound.

### Probability Assessment
The 17% estimate sits between two well-grounded approaches:
- Polymarket-calibrated MC simulation: 24-28%
- Metaculus-based decomposition: ~12%

The calibration against a liquid ($24.7M) Polymarket market is sound. The lower threshold (30 vs 60) makes this easier than Polymarket's question, while the tighter deadline (June 27 vs 30) makes it harder. The MC model consistently shows a ~1.4x ratio. The 17% estimate reasonably weights both approaches and is defensible.

### Counterargument Quality
Strong. The counterargument constructs a coherent case for ~5-8%, citing: the mathematical difficulty of the 7-day MA starting from zero, the Polymarket calibration potentially being misleading (3 extra days are disproportionately valuable), Iran's practical military control as binding constraint, and the precedent of the April 17 "reopening" failure. This is genuinely adversarial.

### Tool Audit
The agent's tool audit doesn't explicitly mention the execute_code timeout, but doesn't claim zero errors either. The 1 error out of 57 calls was a timeout on a calibration grid search that the agent then ran successfully with reduced parameters. Minor issue, not materially misleading.

### Confidence Assessment
0.40 confidence is well-calibrated given the 12-28% range between estimation methods and the deep uncertainty about IRGC compliance and ramp-up speed.

### Overall
No hallucinated evidence, no wrong-direction factors, no resolution misalignment, strong counterargument, well-calibrated confidence. The probability of 17% is reasonable and well-supported by the evidence chain.
